comparisons.bad_example: Our launch went badly. Read on to find out why.
comparisons.good_example: At 9:04 AM we thought launch day was over. At 9:11 AM one customer email changed our whole roadmap.
comparisons.annotation: How to use it: start with a specific moment, then pause before the turning point. Promise a real lesson, not just drama.

comparisons.good_example: Then the buyer asked one question we were not prepared for.
comparisons.annotation: How to use it: place tiny open loops between paragraphs. Keep each one concrete so readers naturally continue.

comparisons.bad_example: Most creators get this wrong, and the answer will shock you.
comparisons.good_example: Most creators lose readers in line two. The fix is not better adjectives—it is one structural move.
comparisons.annotation: How to use it: name a real problem, hint at a counterintuitive solution, then close the loop quickly with a practical takeaway.

before_after.before: I have a crazy story for you.
before_after.after: He almost signed at noon. At 12:07 PM he paused on one sentence in the proposal.
before_after.annotation: Better because it creates a scene with time and stakes.

before_after.after: Open one loop per section, then close it within 3-5 lines so trust goes up instead of down.
before_after.annotation: Better because it explains exactly how to apply curiosity.

before_after.before: This is the biggest storytelling mistake.
before_after.after: If the tension is vague, readers assume the payoff will be vague too—and they bounce.
before_after.annotation: Better because it connects the hook to reader behavior.

playground.base_text: I help experienced professionals communicate their expertise online.
